children.name: Hirundo
children.description: Barn swallows — the most familiar swallows on Earth and one of the most widespread birds in the world. The barn swallow (ladusvala) is a summer visitor of immense cultural significance in Sweden: its arrival in late April or early May is celebrated as a defining moment of the Nordic year. Swallows have lived alongside humans for millennia, nesting in barns, stables, and buildings and feeding on the insects attracted to livestock — a mutualistic relationship that has made them simultaneously dependent on and beloved by farming communities. Swallows are aerial masters, capable of sustained flight speeds exceeding 70 km/h and catching all their food on the wing. They are long-distance migrants, wintering in sub-Saharan Africa and covering up to 10,000 km each way.

children.name: Hirundo rustica
children.description: The ladusvala — Sweden's most beloved summer visitor. Nests in barns and farm buildings; pair bonds are maintained across years with the same mate at the same nest. Long forked tail distinguishes it from all other Swedish swallows. Population declining across Europe due to loss of insect prey from agricultural intensification.

children.name: Delichon
children.description: House martins — compact, blue-black and white swallows that build dome-shaped mud nests attached to cliff faces and — most famously — under the eaves of houses. The common house martin (hussvala) is a familiar summer visitor to Swedish towns and villages, and established colonies under roof eaves can be decades old. House martins collect mud for nest construction from puddles and stream banks, sometimes travelling considerable distances from their nesting site. They are more colonial than barn swallows, often building nests immediately adjacent to one another. Despite their name and intimate association with human buildings, relatively little is known about their winter quarters in sub-Saharan Africa.

children.name: Delichon urbicum
children.description: The hussvala — a familiar sight hawking insects above Swedish towns. White rump patch distinguishes it in flight from all other Swedish swallows. Builds mud nests under roof eaves; established colony sites may be used for generations. Declining in many European countries due to insect declines and renovation of old buildings.

children.name: Riparia
children.description: Sand martins — the smallest and brownest of Sweden's swallows, colonial nesters in sandy riverbanks, sand quarries, and sea cliffs. Each pair excavates a tunnel 30–90 cm deep in bare sand, ending in a nesting chamber — a feat of engineering for a bird weighing just 13 grams. Sand martin colonies can number thousands of pairs and create dramatic, buzzing spectacles when the birds return to their colony after feeding. They are among the earliest summer migrants to arrive in Sweden in spring, and their presence at a colony site can be assessed instantly by the dark rings of nest holes peppering the sand face.

children.name: Riparia riparia
children.description: The backsvala — Sweden's earliest returning swallow. Colonial burrow-nester in riverbanks and sand quarries. One of the first migrants at Swedish spring seawatching points, often appearing in late March. Population fluctuates with Sahelian drought conditions in its African wintering grounds.

children.name: Ptyonoprogne
children.description: Crag martins — robust, earth-coloured swallows of rocky mountains and gorges. The Eurasian crag martin nests on cliff faces and rocky outcrops, plastering a mud cup to vertical rock surfaces in locations completely inaccessible to terrestrial predators. They are resident year-round in southern Europe's mountain ranges, one of the few swallows that don't make long-distance migrations. In Sweden, crag martins are rare vagrants rather than breeding birds, occasionally seen along coastal cliffs in autumn. Their flight is more leisurely and gliding than other swallows, matching the thermal-soaring conditions of mountain environments.

children.name: Ptyonoprogne rupestris
children.description: A resident cliff-nesting swallow of southern European mountains. Rarely recorded in Sweden — an exciting vagrant. Plumage is a uniform brown unlike the contrasting patterns of other European swallows. White spots visible on spread tail in flight.
